title: Megan Wroe and the Bolton bassline revival
author: Allastair Voss
description: Megan Wroe is from Bolton and produces UK garage that sounds like it remembers what UK garage was supposed to feel like.
image_alt: Megan Wroe and the Bolton bassline revival
body:
Megan Wroe is from Bolton and produces UK garage that sounds like it remembers what UK garage was supposed to feel like. She sings on her own tracks. She writes them. The five-track LOVESTEP EP arrived in February on Locked On and it did the thing the genre has needed for a decade: it gave the bassline a person to belong to.

[spotify:artist/2wIBVipx8Fv5orv4uzPW4C]

"See Thru" was the first single. It opens like a 2003 club record and then the vocal arrives and it is unmistakably 2026, confident, conversational, slightly bored in a way that reads as cool rather than disengaged. "Burning For You" with Prozak is the EP's emotional centre, a lovesick garage cut that swings on a bassline you can taste. "Ecstasy" is the one the BBC Radio 1 evening shows have been pulling for.

## What the EP Actually Sounds Like

The production on LOVESTEP is worth describing with some precision because the discourse around UK garage revival tends to flatten everything into the same nostalgic reference. Wroe is not making a museum piece. The drum programming on "See Thru" has the characteristic shuffled two-step feel, the off-kilter swing that makes the groove feel like it is permanently about to tip over, but the mix is contemporary. The low end sits in a register that is less about rattling speakers in a 2002 club and more about how bass translates through earbuds, through the small speakers that are now where most people encounter music for the first time.

That is a meaningful production decision. Revivalists who chase the exact sonic signature of the original era tend to make records that sound like they are arriving from somewhere else, like transmissions from a period rather than music from now. Wroe is working with the structural logic of UK garage, the rhythm, the space, the way a vocal sits on top of a track rather than inside it, and applying that logic to a contemporary mix aesthetic. The songs feel familiar and current at the same time, which is much harder to achieve than either alone.

"Burning For You" with Prozak operates differently from the surrounding material. The tempo drops slightly and the arrangement opens up, creating more air around the vocal. Wroe's delivery on that track is less conversational and more plainly emotional, the kind of singing that does not perform vulnerability but simply has it. The bassline that drives the track is the EP's best moment of pure production craft, a rolling figure that locks into the kick drum in a way that creates a physical sensation before the brain has caught up.

## What She Is Doing

[img:/assets/images/artists/megan-wroe-lovestep-bolton-garage.jpg]

The trick is that she is not pretending to be anyone else. UK garage in the streaming era has had no shortage of revivalists. Most of them sing in someone else's voice and hope the bass covers for them. They hear the genre as style rather than substance, which means the clothes fit but the person wearing them is somewhere else. Wroe sings in her own voice, with her own phrasing, and the songs carry the weight of actual perspective rather than curated affect.

"Closer 2 U" and "Back 2 Tha Start," older singles that pre-date the LOVESTEP era, already had this quality. The production on those tracks is slightly rawer, but the vocal character is already fully formed. Wroe did not develop a voice in the process of making the EP. She arrived with one and the EP gave it a proper stage.

"Ecstasy" deserves specific attention because it is the track most likely to introduce Wroe to listeners who do not follow the UK garage space closely. The hook is immediate in a way that the other tracks deliberately resist. There is a pop instinct at work there that does not compromise the production logic but does remove some of the more genre-specific structural quirks. It is the right track to lead with in a radio context without being the best argument for what Wroe can do. The best argument is "Burning For You."

## Bolton and the Geography of British Club Music

The fact that Wroe is from Bolton rather than London is part of the story and also mostly irrelevant to the music, which is worth saying because British music criticism has a tendency to over-invest in provincial origin stories. What Bolton does provide is distance from the scene's centre of gravity, which means Wroe's relationship to UK garage is that of someone who came to it through listening rather than through proximity to its original cultural infrastructure. The music is something she chose rather than something she absorbed by accident of location.

That kind of deliberate relationship with a genre often produces more considered work than the insider version. When you have had to seek something out rather than having it handed to you by circumstance, you tend to understand it more completely, to hear what it is actually doing structurally rather than what it does socially.

## The Wider Picture

Locked On has been quietly reassembling the UK garage ecosystem for two years. The label's catalogue is a record of taste operating with real editorial focus. Wroe is the artist around whom that ecosystem most obviously orbits now, the one whose work suggests where the label's ambitions are pointed.

She is twenty-something, she produces, she sings, she DJs, and the songs work outside the rave. That last point is the one most UK garage revivals miss entirely. The original scene produced records that were inseparable from their live context, tracks that needed a room and a sound system and a crowd to function. Wroe's songs work on headphones at noon as well as on a system at 2am. The emotional content travels independently of the context. That is the quality that will determine whether LOVESTEP is a well-regarded EP in a small genre or the beginning of something with a wider trajectory.

Start with "See Thru." Stay for "Burning For You." Come back to "Ecstasy" when you want to understand what a pop instinct looks like when it is working in service of the music rather than against it.

Allastair Voss
